narrative.intro: The kingdom of Aquaria has lost its rain. The Water Sage hands you a glowing flask: "Follow a single drop of water through its great journey, and the skies will remember how to rain." Your quest begins at the Sunlit Sea.
narrative.outro: Thunder rolls — gentle, welcome thunder. Rain falls on Aquaria for the first time in a year. The Water Sage bows: "You followed the drop through sky and stone. The cycle is whole again, and so is our kingdom."
perLevel: At the Sunlit Sea, the air shimmers with heat. Your drop begins to feel strangely light...
High above the clouds gather in the Mist Peaks, where the cold air bites and droplets huddle together.
The sky grows heavy and dark over the Stormfall Valley. Something is about to fall...
Deep beneath the Castle of Streams, the final guardian asks where all water finally returns.

teaching.text: The sun heats water in oceans, lakes and rivers. Some of that liquid water turns into water vapor — an invisible gas — and rises into the air. This is called evaporation.
Plants join in too: they release water vapor from tiny pores in their leaves. That part is called transpiration.

options: Liquid water turning into a gas
A gas turning into liquid water
items.explanation: Evaporation is liquid water gaining enough heat energy to become water vapor, a gas that rises into the air.
hints: Think about what happens to a puddle on a hot, sunny day — where does the water go?
It is the step where water LEAVES a surface and rises into the air. Two options describe water staying liquid or solid.

items.prompt: What provides most of the energy that drives evaporation on Earth?
items.explanation: Sunlight is the engine of the water cycle: its heat gives water molecules the energy to escape as vapor.
hints: Evaporation needs HEAT energy. Which of these actually heats the water?
Cross out the two that move water around but do not warm it much.

items.prompt: Water vapor is water in which state of matter?
items.explanation: Water vapor is the gas state of water — invisible, light, and able to rise high into the atmosphere.
hints: Vapor floats up into the air and you cannot see it. Which state behaves like that?
It is not solid like ice and not liquid like rain. Two options remain — pick the one found in air.

items.prompt: Where does MOST of Earth's evaporation happen?
items.explanation: Oceans are HUGE — they cover most of our planet. So they give off far more vapor than all lakes and rivers put together.
hints: Think about which water body covers the largest area of the planet.
Rivers and pools are tiny compared to the biggest option here.

items.prompt: Plants release water vapor from their leaves. What is this process called?
items.explanation: Transpiration is water vapor escaping through tiny pores in leaves — a leafy cousin of evaporation.
hints: The teach card mentioned a special word for the plant version of evaporation.
One option means breathing and one means rain — neither is about leaves releasing vapor.

teaching.text: As water vapor rises, the air around it gets colder. The cold makes the vapor turn back into tiny liquid droplets. This is condensation — the opposite of evaporation.
Billions of those tiny droplets and ice crystals clump together around specks of dust. That is what a cloud actually is.

options: Water vapor turning into liquid droplets
items.explanation: Condensation is vapor cooling down and becoming tiny liquid droplets — evaporation running in reverse.
hints: It is the OPPOSITE of evaporation. Which option reverses it?
Look for the option where a gas becomes a liquid, not the other way round.

items.prompt: Why does water vapor condense as it rises high into the sky?
options: The air up high is colder
The air up high is hotter
items.explanation: Temperature drops as you climb. Cold air slows water molecules down until they stick together as droplets.
hints: Think of mountain tops — are they warmer or cooler than the beach?
Condensation needs the vapor to LOSE heat. Which option takes heat away?

options: Tiny water droplets and ice crystals
items.explanation: A cloud is billions of tiny liquid droplets and ice crystals — vapor is invisible, so what you SEE is liquid and ice.
hints: Vapor is invisible. If you can see a cloud, what must it contain?
Two options are not even made of water — cross those out first.

items.prompt: On a cold morning you find water drops on grass that did not come from rain. What formed them?
items.explanation: Dew is condensation happening at ground level: night air cools, and vapor turns to liquid right on cool surfaces.
hints: The night air cooled down. What does cooling do to the vapor touching the grass?
It is the same process that builds clouds, just happening on the ground.

teaching.text: Inside a cloud, droplets bump and merge, growing bigger and heavier. When they are too heavy for the air to hold, they fall. Any falling water is called precipitation.
Precipitation has many costumes: rain when it is warm, snow when it is freezing, sleet and hail in between.

options: Water falling from clouds to the ground
Water rising from the sea as vapor
items.explanation: Precipitation is any water — rain, snow, sleet or hail — falling from clouds down to Earth.
hints: The word covers rain, snow, sleet AND hail. What do all four do?
Two options describe water going UP or staying put. You want the one going DOWN.

items.prompt: Why do droplets eventually fall out of a cloud?
options: They merge until they are too heavy
The cloud gets bored of them
items.explanation: Droplets collide and merge until gravity wins over the rising air — then down they come as precipitation.
hints: Think about weight. What happens as tiny drops keep bumping and joining?
The real answer is about gravity beating the air that holds drops up.

items.prompt: It is freezing cold in the clouds AND on the ground. Which precipitation is most likely?
items.explanation: When it's freezing all the way down, the water stays frozen as soft icy flakes — that's snow!
hints: Freezing means water turns solid. What soft, white, frozen thing falls from clouds?
One option is a gas and one forms on grass — neither falls from a freezing cloud.

items.prompt: Which list contains ONLY forms of precipitation?
items.explanation: Rain, snow, sleet and hail all FALL from clouds. Fog, dew, thunder and lightning don't fall as water.
hints: Precipitation must actually fall from a cloud to the ground.
Thunder and lightning are not water at all — cross out that list, then check the rest for floaters like fog.

items.prompt: Hail forms when storm winds toss drops up and down through freezing air. What does each trip add?
items.explanation: Each ride through freezing air adds a new icy layer, like an onion — that's why big hailstones have rings.
hints: Cut a hailstone open and you see rings, like an onion. What makes a ring each trip?
The freezing air COATS the stone with something each time it goes up.

teaching.text: Fallen water gathers — in oceans, lakes and rivers, or sinking underground into soil and rock as groundwater. This stage is called collection.
Then the sun shines again and the journey restarts. The water cycle has no finish line: evaporation → condensation → precipitation → collection, around and around for billions of years.

items.prompt: Where does rainwater that soaks into the soil end up?
options: It floats back up at night
items.explanation: Water seeps through soil and rock and is stored underground — wells and springs tap this hidden supply.
hints: The teach card named the hidden water under our feet.
Water never vanishes from the cycle — and it certainly doesn't melt rock.

items.prompt: Which is the correct order of the water cycle, starting from the sea?
items.explanation: Up as vapor, into clouds, down as rain, gathered in seas and soil — then the loop begins again.
hints: Start where the sun lifts water UP from the sea. Which step is that?
After the vapor rises it must become clouds BEFORE anything can fall.

items.prompt: The water you drank today could have been drunk by a dinosaur. Why is that actually true?
options: Water keeps cycling and is never used up
New water arrives from space daily
items.explanation: Earth recycles roughly the same water endlessly through the cycle — today's rain is ancient water on another lap.
hints: Think about whether the cycle ever destroys water or just moves it.
Earth gets almost no new water — so what must be happening to the old water?

items.prompt: A puddle dries up at noon, clouds gather at 3pm, and it rains at 6pm. Which stage happened at 3pm?
items.explanation: Clouds gathering IS condensation — vapor cooling into visible droplets. Drying was evaporation; rain was precipitation.
hints: Match each time to a stage: drying, clouds forming, rain falling.
The 3pm event is about clouds FORMING, not water rising or falling.
